rationale.R1.1: The delegation is entirely a thesis-and-contribution-hierarchy operation. The artifact currently asserts two competing primary contributions (the causal DiD estimate and CrowdAudit) with an ambiguous priority signal ('first contribution' vs. 'central contribution'); reframing requires reconstructing the gap statement, repositioning which finding anchors the paper's identity, and deciding how the hybrid-agent claim relates to empirical vs. methodological work. Failure to get this right invalidates the delegation.
rationale.R1.2: Any reframe that shuffles contribution primacy must preserve the inferential chain: the DiD estimate motivates the detection tool, which motivates the reconceptualization of the crowdworker. If the conceptual ordering changes, the logical connectives between premises and conclusions must be checked for coherence—e.g., the 'era-ending' claim cannot load-bear the intro if it is downstream of both empirical and methodological results.
rationale.R1.5: Narrative arc is structurally entangled with conceptual framing here: whichever contribution is made primary will govern what the reader encounters first (identification strategy vs. detection framework vs. theoretical reconceptualization), so reframing the story necessarily repropagates into section sequencing and arc tension.
